subject: Chemistry
title: Acid Rain: Sulfur in the Sky
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Acid Rain: Sulfur in the Sky.

hook.text: In the 1970s, Scandinavian lakes died. Fish vanished. The cause: sulfur dioxide from British coal plants, hundreds of km away.

buildup.text: Burning coal releases SO₂ and NOₓ. These gases dissolve in rainwater, forming sulfuric and nitric acid.

discovery.text: Acid rain drops lake pH below 5.0. At that level, fish eggs can't develop and aluminum leaches from soil, killing roots.

twist.text: Tall smokestacks built to reduce local pollution just sent the gases higher—spreading acid rain across borders.

climax.text: Cap-and-trade programs cut US SO₂ emissions by 90% since 1990. Acid rain is now largely solved in developed nations.

punchline.text: Coal smoke plus rainwater equals acid. Tall stacks exported the problem, policy fixed it.

quiz.question: How did tall smokestacks make acid rain worse?
options: They sent pollutants higher into the atmosphere, spreading acid rain across borders
They increased the total amount of sulfur dioxide produced
They mixed factory gases into a more acidic combination
